title: Quantum Sufficiency for Self-Adjoint Statistical Models via Likelihood-Type Operators on Real $*$-Subalgebras and Real Jordan Algebras
abstract: We develop a theory of quantum sufficiency on real *-subalgebras and real Jordan algebras. In contrast to the conventional formulation, which is based on families of states, complex completely positive coarse-grainings, and Radon-Nikodym cocycles associated with faithful reference states, our framework allows models consisting of general self-adjoint operators, including derivatives of states. Within this framework, square-root likelihood ratios and symmetric logarithmic derivatives arise naturally as fundamental self-adjoint likelihood-type objects. This makes it possible to treat ordinary quantum statistical models and local quantum statistical structures within a unified setting.   We introduce sufficient real positive maps and show that sufficient complex *-subalgebras, sufficient real *-subalgebras, and sufficient real Jordan algebras correspond respectively to complex completely positive maps, real completely positive maps, and real positive maps. We characterize minimal sufficient real *-subalgebras by the likelihood-ratio set together with rho-modular invariance, and show that the real Jordan algebra generated by the likelihood-ratio set and the projected reference state is the minimal sufficient real Jordan algebra. We also obtain Koashi-Imoto type decompositions for sufficient real *-subalgebras and sufficient real Jordan algebras. Our formulation admits degenerate reference states and separates the likelihood-ratio aspect of sufficiency from its genuinely quantum modular aspect. These results suggest that real Jordan structure provides a natural framework for the statistical aspect of quantum theory beyond the conventional complex *-algebraic setting.
summary: This paper generalizes the theory of quantum sufficiency from the standard complex *-algebraic setting to the more flexible framework of real *-subalgebras and real Jordan algebras. By doing so, it allows for the treatment of statistical models that include non-state self-adjoint operators, such as symmetric logarithmic derivatives, and accommodates reference states that are not strictly positive. The author establishes new characterizations for minimal sufficient algebras and provides a generalized Koashi-Imoto decomposition. Ultimately, the work suggests that real Jordan structures are the natural language for the statistical aspects of quantum theory.
main_problem: The existing theory of quantum sufficiency is limited to complex *-subalgebras and requires faithful reference states, creating a gap when dealing with local quantum statistical structures like SLDs.
main_result: The paper develops a unified theory of quantum sufficiency using real *-subalgebras and real Jordan algebras, allowing for degenerate reference states and the inclusion of self-adjoint operators like SLDs.
method: The author employs algebraic decomposition of real Jordan algebras, operator theory involving rho-modular invariance, and the study of real-linear positive maps and conditional expectations.
model_or_system: The framework considers general self-adjoint statistical models on finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, including both absolutely continuous models and local models (e.g., derivatives of states).
key_observables: Square-root likelihood ratios and symmetric logarithmic derivatives (SLDs).
important_parameters: Finite-dimensional Hilbert space; the distinction between strictly positive and degenerate reference states.
main_assumptions: The framework is presented for finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces and assumes the existence of a supporting operator.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a new framework for sufficiency, establishes a hierarchy of sufficiency (complex, real, and Jordan), characterizes minimal sufficient algebras via likelihood-type objects and modular invariance, provides Koashi-Imlamo type decompositions, and derives bounds for POVM support size.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for quantum estimation and quantum Fisher information, as it provides a more robust mathematical foundation for handling the local statistical structures (SLDs) used in quantum metrology and asymptotic quantum statistics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
